questions.stem: Using the sentence 'THE FAT CAT ATE THE RAT,' the figure contrasts single-base insertion and deletion with a 3-base deletion. Why does the 3-base deletion at the bottom NOT scramble the downstream 'words'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Larger deletions are repaired before translation begins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deletions never affect the reading frame, only insertions do

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three deleted bases are always replaced by a stop codon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing a multiple of three bases preserves the downstream reading frame

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ribosome automatically corrects any deletion of bases

explanation.summary: The genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets. Inserting or deleting a number of bases not divisible by three shifts the reading frame, garbling all downstream codons (a frameshift). Deleting exactly three bases removes one codon but keeps the rest of the frame intact.
explanation.whyCorrect: The genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets. Inserting or deleting a number of bases not divisible by three shifts the reading frame, garbling all downstream codons (a frameshift). Deleting exactly three bases removes one codon but keeps the rest of the frame intact.

questions.stem: The figure shows the same string of numbers read in six different offset registers (+1, +2, +3, -1, -2, -3), with only one highlighted. What genetic concept is being illustrated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The six classes of enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: The six possible reading frames of a nucleotide sequence

choices.label: C
choices.text: The six energy levels of an electron shell

choices.label: D
choices.text: The six stages of the cell cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: The six histone proteins of the nucleosome

explanation.summary: A double-stranded sequence can be translated in three reading frames on each strand (three forward, three reverse), giving six total. Shifting the start point by one base changes which triplets are grouped, illustrating why the correct frame is essential for meaningful translation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A double-stranded sequence can be translated in three reading frames on each strand (three forward, three reverse), giving six total. Shifting the start point by one base changes which triplets are grouped, illustrating why the correct frame is essential for meaningful translation.

questions.stem: The three rows show the identical run of RNA bases grouped into triplets starting at different positions, each yielding a completely different peptide. What principle does this demonstrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wobble pairing changes the amino acid at every codon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Introns must be removed before any codon can be read

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reading frame determines which amino acids a sequence encodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: RNA can be translated in both directions simultaneously

choices.label: E
choices.text: Each amino acid is specified by a single base

explanation.summary: Because codons are read as non-overlapping triplets, the point at which grouping begins (the reading frame) dictates the entire peptide sequence. The same bases read in a different frame specify an entirely different set of amino acids.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because codons are read as non-overlapping triplets, the point at which grouping begins (the reading frame) dictates the entire peptide sequence. The same bases read in a different frame specify an entirely different set of amino acids.

questions.stem: In the leftmost column an AAG->AAA DNA change still yields the amino acid Lys. Why does this base substitution have no effect on the protein product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: tRNA cannot recognize a mutated codon and skips it

choices.label: B
choices.text: The genetic code is degenerate, so the new codon is synonymous and encodes the same amino acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: The substitution created a stop codon that was ignored

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mutation was repaired before translation occurred

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ribosome inserts the original amino acid by default

explanation.summary: Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (degeneracy of the code), often through third-position wobble. A substitution that still codes for the original residue is a silent (synonymous) mutation and leaves the protein sequence unchanged, unlike the nonsense and missense changes in the other columns.
explanation.whyCorrect: Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (degeneracy of the code), often through third-position wobble. A substitution that still codes for the original residue is a silent (synonymous) mutation and leaves the protein sequence unchanged, unlike the nonsense and missense changes in the other columns.
